content:
# Revelation Chapter 1 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, page 278 | **Source Image:** REV_001_page_001.jpg

1. The revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave to him to show to his servants the things that must come to pass soon; and he made it known by sending his angel to his servant John,

2. who bore witness to the word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ — all that he saw.

3. Blessed is the one who reads aloud, and blessed are those who hear the words of this prophecy and keep the things written in it, for the time is near.

4. John, to the seven churches in Asia: Grace to you and peace from him who is and who was and who is coming, and from the seven spirits before his throne,

5. and from Jesus Christ, the faithful witness, the firstborn of the dead, and the ruler of the kings of the earth. To him who loves us and has freed us from our sins by his blood,

6. and made us a kingdom, priests to his God and Father — to him be glory and dominion unto the ages of the ages. Amen.

7. Behold, he is coming with the clouds, and every eye shall see him, even those who pierced him, and all the tribes of the earth shall beat their breasts in grief because of him. Yes indeed. Amen.

8. "I am the Alpha and the Omega," says the Lord God, "who is and who was and who is coming — the Almighty."

9. I, John, your brother and fellow partaker in the tribulation and kingdom and patient endurance in Jesus, was on the island called Patmos on account of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus.

10. I was in the Spirit on the Lord's Day, and I heard behind me a great voice like a trumpet,

11. saying, "What you see, write in a scroll and send it to the seven churches: to Ephesus and to Smyrna and to Pergamum and to Thyatira and to Sardis and to Philadelphia and to Laodicea."

12. And I turned to see the voice that was speaking with me; and having turned, I saw seven golden lampstands,

13. and in the midst of the lampstands one like a son of man, clothed in a robe reaching to the feet and girded at the chest with a golden sash.

14. And his head and his hair were white as white wool, as snow, and his eyes were as a flame of fire,

15. and his feet were like burnished bronze, as refined in a furnace, and his voice was as the sound of many waters.

16. And he had in his right hand seven stars, and out of his mouth proceeded a sharp two-edged sword, and his face was as the sun shining in its full strength.

17. And when I saw him, I fell at his feet as though dead. And he laid his right hand upon me, saying, "Fear not; I am the First and the Last,

18. and the Living One. I was dead, and behold, I am alive unto the ages of the ages, and I have the keys of Death and of Hades.

19. Write therefore the things which you have seen, and the things which are, and the things which are about to come to pass after these things.

20. The mystery of the seven stars which you saw in my right hand, and the seven golden lampstands: the seven stars are the angels of the seven churches, and the seven lampstands are the seven churches."

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

The three translators showed strong agreement on the overall structure and meaning of Revelation 1. All three correctly identified the Sinaiticus reading LYSANTI ("freed/loosed") in v.5 rather than the Byzantine variant LOUSANTI ("washed"), and all preserved the anarthrous "son of man" in v.13 as a Danielic allusion. The manuscript image (page 278, four columns, scriptio continua with nomina sacra) confirms the text as read by all three translators. The heading APOKALYPSIS IOANOU is visible at the top of the page. All three translators faithfully rendered the core theological content: the divine triad formula (v.4, 8), the kingdom-priests construction (v.6), and the self-identification of the risen Christ (vv.17-18).

---
## Disagreement Log

**Verse 1 — "revelation" vs. "unveiling":**
- EMET: "revelation" (with transliterated names: "Iesous Christos")
- LOGOS: "revelation"
- PNEUMA: "unveiling" with expansive paraphrase ("This is the unveiling of Jesus Christ -- a revelation God gave him")
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "revelation" as the established English equivalent of apokalypsis, following EMET and LOGOS. PNEUMA's "unveiling" is etymologically accurate but departs from convention. The simpler opening structure of LOGOS was preferred over PNEUMA's double-phrase rendering. Names rendered in standard English (Jesus Christ) following LOGOS and PNEUMA rather than EMET's transliterations.

**Verse 1 — "shortly/soon/in swiftness":**
- EMET: "in swiftness"
- LOGOS: "shortly"
- PNEUMA: "soon"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "soon" as the most natural English rendering of EN TACHEI while remaining faithful. "In swiftness" is hyper-literal; "shortly" is slightly archaic.

**Verse 1 — "servants/slaves":**
- EMET: "slaves"
- LOGOS: "servants"
- PNEUMA: "servants"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "servants" (2-1 majority). DOULOS can mean both; "servants" is more appropriate for the covenantal relationship in view while "slaves" captures the social reality. In this theological context, "servants" prevails.

**Verse 2 — "testified/bore witness/testifies":**
- EMET: "testified" (past tense)
- LOGOS: "bore witness" (past tense)
- PNEUMA: "testifies" (present tense: "who now testifies")
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "bore witness" following LOGOS. The Greek aorist EMARTYRESEN is past tense; PNEUMA's present tense departs from the source.

**Verse 3 — Structural expansion:**
- EMET: "Blessed [is] the [one] reading and the [ones] hearing"
- LOGOS: "Blessed is the one who reads aloud, and blessed are those who hear"
- PNEUMA: "Blessed is the one who reads these prophetic words aloud, and blessed are those who hear them and take to heart what is written here, for the time of fulfillment is near"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Followed LOGOS closely. PNEUMA's "take to heart" interprets TEROUNTES ("keeping/guarding") more freely than warranted; "the time of fulfillment is near" adds "fulfillment" not present in the Greek.

**Verse 4 — "churches" vs. "assemblies":**
- EMET: "assemblies"
- LOGOS: "churches"
- PNEUMA: "churches"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "churches" (2-1 majority). While EKKLESIAI literally means "assemblies" (as EMET notes), "churches" is the received English rendering for these specific communities and aids reader recognition.

**Verse 4 — "who is to come" vs. "who is coming":**
- EMET: "the [one who is] coming"
- LOGOS: "who is to come"
- PNEUMA: "who is coming"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "who is coming" — a present participle (HO ERCHOMENOS) best rendered as ongoing/imminent. LOGOS's "is to come" is slightly more distant in feel. EMET and PNEUMA agree on the participial force.

**Verse 5 — "freed" vs. "loosed":**
- EMET: "loosed"
- LOGOS: "freed"
- PNEUMA: "freed"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "freed" (2-1 majority). Both are valid for LYSANTI; "freed" communicates more directly in modern English while remaining accurate.

**Verse 6 — "might" vs. "dominion":**
- EMET: "might" (TO KRATOS)
- LOGOS: "dominion"
- PNEUMA: "dominion"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "dominion" (2-1 majority). KRATOS conveys sovereign ruling power; "dominion" captures this better than "might."

**Verse 6 — "into the ages" vs. "unto the ages" vs. "forever and ever":**
- EMET: "into the ages of the ages"
- LOGOS: "unto the ages of the ages"
- PNEUMA: "forever and ever"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "unto the ages of the ages" following LOGOS, preserving the distinctive Hebraic idiom while being slightly more natural than EMET's "into." PNEUMA's "forever and ever" loses the distinctive phrasing.

**Verse 7 — "mourn/beat their breasts/beat [their breasts]":**
- EMET: "will beat [their breasts] over him all the tribes of the earth"
- LOGOS: "all the tribes of the earth shall mourn over him"
- PNEUMA: "all the peoples of the earth will beat their chests in grief because of him"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted a hybrid: "all the tribes of the earth shall beat their breasts in grief because of him." KOPSONTAI (from KOPTO) denotes physical mourning gestures, not merely grief. EMET and PNEUMA preserve this; LOGOS's "mourn" loses the physicality. "Tribes" (PHYLAI) preserved over PNEUMA's "peoples."

**Verse 7 — "Yes, amen" vs. "Even so. Amen" vs. "Yes indeed. Amen":**
- EMET: "Yes, amen"
- LOGOS: "Even so. Amen"
- PNEUMA: "Yes indeed. Amen"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "Yes indeed. Amen" — it captures the emphatic double affirmation (NAI, AMEN) with appropriate weight.

**Verse 8 — Quotation formatting:**
- EMET: No quotation marks
- LOGOS: No quotation marks
- PNEUMA: Quotation marks around the divine speech
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted quotation marks following PNEUMA, as they clarify the direct divine speech for the reader.

**Verse 9 — "fellow-partaker/fellow partaker/partner":**
- EMET: "fellow-partaker"
- LOGOS: "fellow partaker"
- PNEUMA: "partner"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "fellow partaker" following LOGOS. SYNKOINONOS specifically means one who shares in something together; "partner" (PNEUMA) is too generic.

**Verse 9 — "I came to be" vs. "was":**
- EMET: "I came to be in the island"
- LOGOS: "was on the island"
- PNEUMA: "I was on the island"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "was on the island" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). EGENOMEN literally means "I came to be" but in this context functions as a past-tense locative; natural English requires "was."

**Verse 10 — "in spirit/in the Spirit/caught up in the Spirit":**
- EMET: "in spirit" (lowercase, ambiguous)
- LOGOS: "in the Spirit" (capitalized, with article)
- PNEUMA: "caught up in the Spirit" (interpretive addition)
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "in the Spirit" following LOGOS. The capitalization reflects the likely reference to the Holy Spirit. PNEUMA's "caught up" adds interpretive content not in the Greek EN PNEUMATI.

**Verse 10 — "lordly day/the Lord's day/the Lord's Day":**
- EMET: "the lordly day"
- LOGOS: "the Lord's day"
- PNEUMA: "the Lord's Day"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "the Lord's Day" following PNEUMA's capitalization, as TE KYRIAKE HEMERA is a technical term for the Christian day of worship.

**Verse 10 — "great voice/tremendous voice":**
- EMET: "great voice"
- LOGOS: "great voice"
- PNEUMA: "tremendous voice"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "great voice" (2-1 majority). MEGALEN is straightforwardly "great"; "tremendous" is interpretive intensification.

**Verse 11 — "scroll/book":**
- EMET: "scroll"
- LOGOS: "book"
- PNEUMA: "scroll"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "scroll" (2-1 majority). BIBLION in the first century refers to a scroll, not a codex-style book.

**Verse 12 — "the voice which was speaking with me" vs. "who was speaking to me":**
- EMET/LOGOS: "the voice that/which was speaking with me"
- PNEUMA: "who was speaking to me" (personalizes the voice)
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "the voice that was speaking with me" — preserving the focus on the voice itself, as in the Greek.

**Verse 13 — "breast/chest":**
- EMET: "breasts"
- LOGOS: "chest"
- PNEUMA: "chest"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "chest" (2-1 majority). MASTOIS can mean "breasts/chest"; "chest" is clearer in English for the location of the sash.

**Verse 13 — "belt/sash":**
- EMET: "belt"
- LOGOS: "sash"
- PNEUMA: "sash"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "sash" (2-1 majority). ZONEN in this context describes a broad cloth band, better captured by "sash."

**Verse 14 — "as a flame of fire" vs. "blazed like fire":**
- EMET: "as a flame of fire"
- LOGOS: "as a flame of fire"
- PNEUMA: "blazed like fire"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "as a flame of fire" (2-1 majority). Preserves the simile structure of the Greek HOS PHLOX PYROS.

**Verse 15 — "fine brass/burnished bronze":**
- EMET: "fine brass"
- LOGOS: "burnished bronze"
- PNEUMA: "bronze refined" (paraphrase)
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "burnished bronze" following LOGOS. CHALKOLIBANON is a rare word; "burnished bronze" conveys both the material and the luminous quality. EMET's "fine brass" is acceptable but less evocative.

**Verse 15 — "sound/roar/voice":**
- EMET: "sound of many waters"
- LOGOS: "sound of many waters"
- PNEUMA: "thundered like the roar of many waters"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "the sound of many waters" (2-1 majority). PHONE HYDATON POLLON is "sound of many waters." PNEUMA's "thundered like the roar" adds interpretive force not in the text.

**Verse 16 — "shines in the power of it" vs. "shining in its full strength" vs. "blazing at full strength":**
- EMET: "as the sun shines in the power of it"
- LOGOS: "as the sun shining in its full strength"
- PNEUMA: "like the sun blazing at full strength"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "as the sun shining in its full strength" following LOGOS. PHAINEI EN TE DYNAMEI AUTOU — "shining in its power/strength." LOGOS captures both accuracy and force.

**Verse 17 — "Do not fear/Fear not/Do not be afraid":**
- EMET: "Do not fear"
- LOGOS: "Fear not"
- PNEUMA: "Do not be afraid"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "Fear not" following LOGOS — it carries a weightier, more solemn register appropriate to divine speech while faithfully rendering ME PHOBOU.

**Verse 18 — "Hades" vs. "the grave":**
- EMET: "the Hades"
- LOGOS: "Hades"
- PNEUMA: "the grave"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "Hades" following LOGOS. The Greek HADOU is a proper noun for the realm of the dead. PNEUMA's "the grave" flattens the cosmological meaning; EMET's article ("the Hades") is awkward in English.

**Verse 18 — "living I am" vs. "I am living" vs. "I am alive":**
- EMET: "living I am into the ages of the ages"
- LOGOS: "I am living unto the ages of the ages"
- PNEUMA: "I am alive forever and ever"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "I am alive unto the ages of the ages" — combining PNEUMA's natural "I am alive" with LOGOS's preservation of the Hebraic temporal formula.

**Verse 19 — "are about to come to be" vs. "are about to come to pass" vs. "will happen":**
- EMET: "about to come to be after these things"
- LOGOS: "about to come to pass after these things"
- PNEUMA: "what will happen after this"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "about to come to pass after these things" following LOGOS. MELLEI GENESTHAI META TAUTA — the phrase "about to" preserves the imminence of MELLEI.

**Verse 20 — "upon the right hand" vs. "in my right hand":**
- EMET: "upon the right [hand] of me"
- LOGOS: "in my right hand"
- PNEUMA: "in my right hand"
- SOPHIA's resolution: Adopted "in my right hand" (2-1 majority).

— SOPHIA
